summary: Epic He's Gone jam, scorching keyboards, tight second‑set marathon
blurb: The show shines with an explosive He's Gone > Jam and a soaring China Cat Sunflower > I Know You Rider transition. Brent Mydland’s magical keyboards and Jerry Garcia’s bright guitar on Standing On The Moon stand out. Overall the second set delivers relentless energy despite a few weaker first‑set numbers.
review: July 6, 1990 at Louisville’s Cardinal Stadium captured one of the Dead’s late‑summer firestorms. From the opening “Hell In A Bucket” through a solid first set, the band built momentum that exploded in the second set: a blistering China Cat Sunflower > I Know You Rider segue launched into a marathon of jams, highlighted by an epic He\'s Gone > Jam and a thunderous Space > Truckin' climax. Jerry Garcia’s tone stayed crystal‑clear all night—bright on Standing On The Moon, inventive with a MIDI synth solo on Ramble On Rose—and he led the band through those soaring peaks. Brent Mydland, despite being under the weather, delivered magical keyboard textures that lifted songs like Ramble On Rose and Peggy‑O, while Phil Lesh’s bass remained crystal‑clear, even slipping into jazzy moments during Around & Around. The dual drumming of Mickey Hart and Bill Kreutzmann powered the drums‑to‑space segment with pounding percussive colors. Though a few first‑set numbers (Easy To Love You, Desolation Row) fell flat, the overall performance was tight, energetic, and memorable—making this show a standout entry in the 1990 tour.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Four recordings praised the show as 'epic', 'magical', and highlighted multiple standout jams (He\'s Gone > Jam, China>Rider, Standing On The Moon). Mixed reviews only noted occasional weak songs but never criticized overall performance, yielding a strong positive sentiment.

best_recording.reason: Miller’s crystal‑clear soundboard capture preserves every nuance of the epic jams and provides the most balanced, drop‑free listening experience.

key_highlights: He\'s Gone > Jam and extended China Cat Sunflower > I Know You Rider transition dominate the second set.
Brent Mydland’s magical keyboards shine despite illness, especially on Ramble On Rose and Peggy‑O.
Space > Truckin' and the drums/space segment deliver a high‑energy climax.

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia delivered a scorching performance, leading the monumental He's Gone > Jam, sounding bright on Standing On The Moon and adding a memorable MIDI synth solo on Ramble On Rose; his tone stayed crystal‑clear all night despite the heat.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh’s bass was consistently crystal‑clear, providing a solid foundation and slipping into a jazzy groove during Around & Around.
band_performance.Bob: Weir supplied steady rhythm and strong vocal moments, notably the 'Truckin'' skeleton section, anchoring the band throughout.
band_performance.Brent: Mydland’s keyboards were a highlight—magical textures and inventive fills—even while battling illness; his vocal on Easy To Love You fell flat but his overall playing shone.
band_performance.Mickey: Hart delivered powerful, beam‑pounding percussion during the drums/space stretch, adding distinctive textures to the jam.
band_performance.Billy: Kreutzmann’s drumming drove the post‑He's Gone jam and kept the energy high through the drums‑to‑space transition.

must_listen_sequences: Goin' Down The Road Feeling Bad
